teens.question: What does the beloved say are like clusters of the vine and like the smell of apples?
teens.option_a: The woman's breasts
teens.option_b: Her lips and her tongue
teens.option_c: The fragrance of her garments
teens.option_d: Her hands and her feet
teens.explanation: Song of Solomon 7:8 — "Thy breasts shall be as clusters of the vine, and the smell of thy nose like apples."

adults.question: How does Song of Solomon 7:10 — "I am my beloved's, and his desire is toward me" — mark the culmination of the three mutual possession formulae in the book (2:16, 6:3, 7:10)?
adults.option_a: The progression moves from claiming him (my beloved is mine, 2:16) to belonging to him first (I am my beloved's, 6:3) to complete self-gift with the addition of his desire toward her (7:10) — each stage deepens self-surrender while gaining assurance of being fully desired
adults.option_b: The three formulae are equivalent statements of the same truth and show no progression
adults.option_c: The formula in 7:10 is spoken by the beloved rather than the woman, reversing the earlier pattern
adults.option_d: The change from mutual to unilateral possession in 7:10 indicates a decline in the relationship's health
adults.explanation: Song of Solomon 2:16 — "My beloved is mine, and I am his." Song of Solomon 6:3 — "I am my beloved's, and my beloved is mine." Song of Solomon 7:10 — "I am my beloved's, and his desire is toward me." The arc moves from possession (mine) to belonging (his) to the discovery of being desired — the highest gift.

adults.question: What is the theological resonance of "his desire is toward me" (Song of Solomon 7:10) given the use of the same word (teshuqah — desire) in Genesis 3:16?
adults.option_a: Genesis 3:16 uses teshuqah to describe the woman's distorted desire for her husband under the curse; Song of Solomon 7:10 inverts this — the man's desire is now described with the same word, suggesting the garden of love in the Song restores what was broken at the Fall
adults.option_b: The word teshuqah is common Hebrew with no deliberate Genesis connection in Song 7:10
adults.option_c: Genesis 3:16 describes God's desire for humanity, not the man's desire for the woman
adults.option_d: The connection to Genesis 3:16 is a later allegorical reading not intended by the original poem
adults.explanation: Genesis 3:16 — "thy desire (teshuqah) shall be to thy husband, and he shall rule over thee." This is the curse: disordered desire and domination. Song of Solomon 7:10 — "his desire (teshuqah) is toward me." The same word now describes the man's desire turned fully toward the woman — the poem envisions love as the reversal of the curse, a restored Edenic relationship.

adults.question: How does the beloved's third wasf in Song of Solomon 7:1-9 differ from the wasf in chapters 4-5 in terms of direction and tone?
adults.option_a: Chapter 7's wasf begins at the feet (how beautiful are thy feet with shoes) and ascends to the head — an upward movement that mirrors the woman's own growing confidence and full presence, culminating in the beloved's desire to climb the palm tree and take hold of its branches
adults.option_b: All three wasfs in the book follow the same head-to-foot direction and show no structural difference
adults.option_c: Chapter 7's wasf begins at the head rather than the feet, making it structurally identical to chapter 4
adults.option_d: The direction of the wasf in chapter 7 is indeterminate since the body parts are listed randomly
adults.explanation: Song of Solomon 7:1 — "How beautiful are thy feet with shoes, O prince's daughter!" The wasf begins at the feet and ascends: feet (v.1), thighs (v.1), navel (v.2), belly (v.2), breasts (v.3-8), neck (v.4), eyes (v.4), nose (v.4), head (v.5), hair (v.5). This ascending movement to the head mirrors the beloved's declaration in verse 8 that he will go up to the palm tree.

adults.question: What does calling the woman "prince's daughter" (bat-nadib, Song of Solomon 7:1) contribute to her characterisation throughout the book?
adults.option_a: It elevates her status from the sun-darkened vineyard keeper of chapter 1 to noble daughter — whether literally or metaphorically, love itself has made her royal, tracing a transformation across the book from social outsider to recognised nobility
adults.option_b: It identifies her as the daughter of a foreign king whom Solomon married for political reasons
adults.option_c: The title is a generic honorific applied to all women in royal court poetry without specific meaning
adults.option_d: It shows that by chapter 7 her identity has been legally changed through the marriage ceremony of chapter 3
adults.explanation: Song of Solomon 7:1 — "How beautiful are thy feet with shoes, O prince's daughter!" Compare Song of Solomon 1:5-6 where she is a tanned vineyard keeper looked down upon. The trajectory from social outsider to bat-nadib (noble daughter/daughter of a prince) enacted through love's transformation is one of the book's quiet themes.

adults.question: What is the significance of the woman taking the initiative to invite the beloved to the countryside in Song of Solomon 7:11-13?
adults.option_a: The woman's active invitation reverses the usual pattern of the male initiating; her agency grows across the book and here she becomes the architect of their meeting — love matures into mutual, active pursuit rather than passive receptivity
adults.option_b: It shows the woman had not yet been formally invited to the king's palace and was suggesting an alternative meeting place
adults.option_c: The woman's initiative is presented as a violation of Israelite custom and the beloved declines in chapter 8
adults.option_d: The rural invitation indicates the couple are unmarried and the vineyard is the only place they can meet
adults.explanation: Song of Solomon 7:11-12 — "Come, my beloved, let us go forth into the field; let us lodge in the villages...there will I give thee my loves." She plans, she invites, she sets the location, and she promises her gift of love. Her growing agency is a thread across the book — from seeking him (ch.3) to being sought (ch.5) to now actively leading (ch.7).

adults.question: How does the mandrake imagery in Song of Solomon 7:13 connect to earlier biblical usage, and what does it contribute to the chapter?
adults.option_a: Mandrakes (dudaim) were fertility plants associated with love-making (Genesis 30:14-16 — Rachel and Leah's bargain); their presence in the garden gives the invitation of verse 12 erotic and fertility overtones while rooting the lovers' relationship in the creation mandate to be fruitful
adults.option_b: Mandrakes are simply pleasant-smelling herbs with no specific biblical or sexual resonance
adults.option_c: The mandrake reference is borrowed from Egyptian garden poetry and has no connection to the Genesis narrative
adults.option_d: Mandrakes were used in temple incense and the reference places the garden visit in a cultic context
adults.explanation: Song of Solomon 7:13 — "The mandrakes give a smell, and at our gates are all manner of pleasant fruits." Genesis 30:14-16 — Reuben found mandrakes (dudaim) in the field and Rachel bargained with Leah for them because of their fertility/love association. The same word dudaim echoes the word for beloved (dod), creating a pun that suffuses the garden invitation with erotic and generative meaning.

adults.question: How does the beloved's description of the woman's walk — "How beautiful are thy feet with shoes, O prince's daughter! The joints of thy thighs are like jewels" (Song of Solomon 7:1) — reflect the social setting of a dance?
adults.option_a: The reference to sandals and the context of "return, return, O Shulamite" from the previous verse suggests a dance (the Mahanaim dance of 6:13) in which the woman's movement, posture, and adorned feet are being admired by an audience
adults.option_b: The verse describes the woman walking to the marketplace and her sandals are mentioned for realism
adults.option_c: The sandal reference identifies her as a Levite since only Levites wore decorated sandals in Israel
adults.option_d: The verse follows a wedding procession where the bride's feet were formally blessed
adults.explanation: Song of Solomon 6:13 — "What will ye see in the Shulamite? As it were the company of two armies (Mahanaim)." Song of Solomon 7:1 — "How beautiful are thy feet with shoes, O prince's daughter!" Mahanaim means two camps and may describe a dance performance. The transition from the "look upon thee" of 6:13 to the admiration of her feet and movement in 7:1 places the wasf in the context of a dance being observed.

adults.question: What does the whole of chapter 7's movement — from a public dance setting (7:1) to an intimate private invitation (7:11-13) — reveal about the book's theology of love's seasons?
adults.option_a: Love has both public and private dimensions: it can be celebrated openly and seen by the community (dance), yet its deepest gifts are reserved for private covenant space (the field and village). True love is neither purely private nor purely public but rhythmically moves between the two
adults.option_b: Chapter 7 shows the relationship deteriorating from public honour to private secrecy
adults.option_c: The movement from public to private marks the legal transition from betrothal to consummation
adults.option_d: The shift is structural only — the public-to-private movement has no theological implication
adults.explanation: Song of Solomon 7:1 — "How beautiful are thy feet with shoes" (public admiration in the dance). Song of Solomon 7:11-13 — "Come, my beloved, let us go forth into the field...there will I give thee my loves" (private invitation). The book throughout holds together love as both publicly honourable and privately intimate — neither reduced to performance nor hidden in shame.

kids.option_d: Keep my gift as a seal upon thy hand
kids.explanation: Song of Solomon 8:6 — "Set me as a seal upon thine heart, as a seal upon thine arm."

kids.option_d: Neither time nor sorrow can cause true love to fade
kids.explanation: Song of Solomon 8:7 — "Many waters cannot quench love, neither can the floods drown it."

teens.question: What does the woman say love is as strong as?

teens.option_c: The sun whose heat none can escape
teens.option_d: The sea whose waters fill all the earth
teens.explanation: Song of Solomon 8:6 — "Love is strong as death; jealousy is cruel as the grave."

teens.option_b: My vineyard is greater than Solomon's because love tends it
teens.option_c: Solomon has all the vineyards of Israel but I have what is mine
teens.option_d: My vineyard yields more fruit than all the vineyards of the king
teens.explanation: Song of Solomon 8:11-12 — "Solomon had a vineyard at Baal-hamon...My vineyard, which is mine, is before me: thou, O Solomon, must have a thousand, and those that keep the fruit thereof two hundred."

teens.question: What does the woman say she would do if she could meet her beloved in the street like a brother, and why does she wish this?

teens.option_b: She would take him to the gate and declare her love before all the elders
teens.option_c: She would give him wine from her vineyard and break bread with him openly
teens.option_d: She would lead him to the king's house and present him to her family
teens.explanation: Song of Solomon 8:1-2 — "O that thou wert as my brother...I would kiss thee; yea, I should not be despised. I would lead thee, and bring thee into my mother's house."

teens.question: What does the beloved say about the woman leaning upon him as she comes up from the wilderness?

teens.option_c: My beloved rises from the desert as one clothed in glory
teens.option_d: I awakened her under the apple tree where her mother travailed
teens.explanation: Song of Solomon 8:5 — "Who is this that cometh up from the wilderness, leaning upon her beloved?"

teens.question: What does the woman say about anyone who tries to buy love with all the substance or wealth of his house?
teens.option_a: It would utterly be contemned or scorned
teens.option_b: It could never be enough to satisfy true love
teens.option_c: Such a man is a fool and knows nothing of love
teens.option_d: He has mistaken riches for what belongs only to the heart
teens.explanation: Song of Solomon 8:7 — "If a man would give all the substance of his house for love, it would utterly be contemned."

adults.question: What is the significance of the seal imagery in Song of Solomon 8:6 — "Set me as a seal upon thine heart, as a seal upon thine arm"?
adults.option_a: In the ancient world a seal (hotam) was pressed into clay or wax to establish identity, ownership, and legal authority; to ask to be set as a seal upon the heart and arm is to ask to be the beloved's defining mark — on the inmost self (heart) and in all outward action (arm)
adults.option_b: The seal imagery is borrowed from legal contracts and has no particular relational or theological resonance
adults.option_c: The seal refers to the signet ring of betrothal that was placed on the woman's hand at the marriage ceremony
adults.option_d: It is a military image: the seal on the arm was worn by soldiers entering battle
adults.explanation: Song of Solomon 8:6 — "Set me as a seal upon thine heart, as a seal upon thine arm." Genesis 38:18 (Judah's signet/hotam) and Haggai 2:23 ("I will make thee as a signet") use the same word for the seal of identity and authority. The request is to be permanently impressed into the beloved's identity — not temporarily but definitively.

adults.question: How does the declaration "love is strong as death" (Song of Solomon 8:6) function as the theological climax of the book, and what does the comparison reveal?
adults.option_a: Death is the one absolute — universal, inevitable, and irresistible; by comparing love's strength to death, the poem declares that love is equally absolute and irresistible. It is not that love defeats death here, but that love is its only rival in absolute power
adults.option_b: The comparison is hyperbole intended only to praise the intensity of romantic feeling
adults.option_c: It means love causes death — the jealousy described in the same verse is a warning against obsessive love
adults.option_d: It is a statement about the love of God for Israel rather than human love between individuals
adults.explanation: Song of Solomon 8:6 — "Love is strong as death; jealousy is cruel as the grave: the coals thereof are coals of fire, which hath a most vehement flame." The Hebrew sheol (grave/realm of death) was understood as the great equaliser, claiming all. To say love is its equal is to say love is among the absolute realities — which is why Paul echoes this in Romans 8:38-39 and John 3:16 grounds the gospel in the same logic.

adults.question: What is the meaning of "the coals thereof are coals of fire, which hath a most vehement flame" (Song of Solomon 8:6), and what does the Hebrew shalhevetyah contribute?
adults.option_a: The Hebrew shalhevetyah (a most vehement flame) may contain the divine name Yah (a shortened form of YHWH) — making this the book's only possible explicit reference to God, suggesting love's fiercest flame partakes of divine fire
adults.option_b: Shalhevetyah is simply a superlative compound meaning the hottest possible flame without any divine name
adults.option_c: The fire imagery is borrowed from the altar of burnt offering and presents love as a sacrifice
adults.option_d: The compound word identifies the flame as the pillar of fire that led Israel in the wilderness
adults.explanation: Song of Solomon 8:6 — "the coals thereof are coals of fire, which hath a most vehement flame (shalhevetyah)." Hebrew lexicographers including Gesenius note that shalhevetyah may be shalhebet + Yah (flame of the LORD), making this either the strongest superlative in the Hebrew language or an explicit claim that love's ultimate fire is nothing less than divine. Either reading is theologically rich.

adults.question: How does the statement "if a man would give all the substance of his house for love, it would utterly be contemned" (Song of Solomon 8:7) function as a theological declaration about love's nature?
adults.option_a: Love cannot be purchased because it belongs to a different order of reality than commercial exchange; it is given freely or not at all. Any attempt to buy it with wealth reveals a fundamental misunderstanding of what love is
adults.option_b: It is a legal principle warning against selling a woman into marriage for a bride price
adults.option_c: It means that wealthy men make poor lovers and the woman is warning Solomon against trying to impress her with gifts
adults.option_d: The verse counsels financial prudence — do not spend your wealth pursuing love because it is unstable
adults.explanation: Song of Solomon 8:7 — "Many waters cannot quench love, neither can the floods drown it: if a man would give all the substance of his house for love, it would utterly be contemned." The verse places love outside the economy of barter. Love is not a commodity. It cannot be destroyed by force (waters) nor acquired by wealth (substance). Its only medium is free, self-giving devotion.

adults.question: How does the vineyard motif in Song of Solomon 8:11-12 bring the book's vineyard imagery to a conclusion, and what does "my vineyard, which is mine, is before me" declare?
adults.option_a: The book opens with the woman unable to keep her own vineyard (1:6); it closes with her asserting that her vineyard — herself — is fully and freely hers to give. The contrast traces the whole arc of the book from lost self-possession to recovered identity
adults.option_b: The vineyard in chapter 8 is a literal farm that the couple has acquired through marriage
adults.option_c: The contrast between Solomon's vineyard and hers is purely financial, comparing the value of rural vs royal property
adults.option_d: The woman's vineyard in chapter 8 is the same vineyard she kept for her brothers in chapter 1, showing she has returned home
adults.explanation: Song of Solomon 1:6 — "mine own vineyard have I not kept." Song of Solomon 8:12 — "My vineyard, which is mine, is before me." The inclusion (bookend) is deliberate: the woman who could not tend her own vineyard has, through the journey of love, come into full ownership of herself — her vineyard is now hers to give rather than taken from her.

adults.question: What does the community's question about the little sister (Song of Solomon 8:8-9) introduce, and how does the woman's response in verse 10 reflect her own completed journey?
adults.option_a: The community debates how to protect a girl not yet ready for love; the woman's response — "I am a wall, and my breasts like towers: then was I in his eyes as one that found favour" — presents herself as the resolution: she became her own wall of integrity and was received with favour, offering the model for her sister
adults.option_b: The little sister passage is a genealogical note identifying the woman's family background
adults.option_c: The community is discussing a property dispute about the sister's inheritance rights
adults.option_d: The woman's response in verse 10 is unrelated to the sister question and addresses her beloved directly
adults.explanation: Song of Solomon 8:8-9 — "We have a little sister...what shall we do for our sister?" Song of Solomon 8:10 — "I am a wall, and my breasts like towers: then was I in his eyes as one that found favour (shalom)." Her own formation — keeping herself as an enclosed garden until love's proper time — is the answer she embodies. She did not remain a locked door (v.9) but a wall that chose to open in its own time.

adults.question: How does the woman's request in Song of Solomon 8:14 — "Make haste, my beloved, and be thou like to a roe or to a young hart upon the mountains of spices" — echo the book's opening and what does ending here reveal about love?
adults.option_a: It echoes Song of Solomon 2:8-9 where the beloved first appeared leaping like a roe; ending the book with the same image suggests love's desire is never fully exhausted — it ends in longing and invitation rather than arrival, depicting love as an ongoing pursuit rather than a completed possession
adults.option_b: The verse shows the relationship has ended since the woman sends the beloved away to the mountains
adults.option_c: The closing verse is a liturgical dismissal, ending the wedding ceremony described in the book
adults.option_d: It indicates the woman has rejected the beloved and the book ends unresolved
adults.explanation: Song of Solomon 2:8-9 — "Behold, he cometh leaping upon the mountains, skipping upon the hills. My beloved is like a roe or a young hart." Song of Solomon 8:14 — "Make haste, my beloved, and be thou like to a roe or to a young hart upon the mountains of spices." The book closes not with satisfied possession but with renewed longing — an eschatological posture that the New Testament appropriates in Revelation 22:20: "Even so, come, Lord Jesus."

adults.question: How does the whole book of Song of Solomon function canonically within the Hebrew Bible, and what does its position in the Writings (Ketuvim) alongside Psalms, Proverbs, and Ecclesiastes suggest about its purpose?
adults.option_a: Positioned among the wisdom literature, the Song offers a theology of embodied, covenantal love as part of God's created order — not a lesser or suspicious topic but wisdom's own engagement with what it means to be fully human. It declares that faithful love between persons reflects something true about the nature of God and creation
adults.option_b: The Song was placed in the Writings accidentally because it fit neither the Law nor the Prophets
adults.option_c: Its canonical position indicates it is primarily historical, recording the actual relationship of Solomon with a named historical figure
adults.option_d: The Song belongs to the Writings only because it was too controversial for the Law or Prophets
adults.explanation: The Song's placement in the Ketuvim alongside Proverbs (which ends with the eshet hayil of Proverbs 31, echoing Ruth 3:11 and Song 3:11) and Qohelet (Ecclesiastes, also Solomonic) creates a wisdom cluster exploring what it means to live well. Rabbi Akiva's verdict — "the Song of Songs is the Holy of Holies" (Mishnah Yadaim 3:5) — understands the book not as erotic literature appended to Scripture but as wisdom's holiest chamber: the place where love, creation, and the divine nature meet.
